era_context: The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) dominates European and global politics, pitting Britain and Prussia against France, Austria, Russia, and Saxony. This is the height of the Enlightenment in Europe, with intellectual ferment in philosophy, natural science, and political theory. The Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi and Yongzheng legacy continues to consolidate control over China and Central Asia. The Ottoman Empire remains a major power but faces internal reform pressures. Colonial rivalries in North America, India, and the Caribbean are intensifying.

events.title: Seven Years' War begins with Prussian invasion of Saxony
events.description: Frederick II of Prussia launches a preemptive invasion of Saxony on 29 August 1756, initiating the European theatre of the Seven Years' War. This aggressive move aims to secure territory and resources before Austria and Russia can fully mobilize. The invasion is rapid and successful initially, but triggers a coalition response that will consume Prussia's resources for seven years. The war becomes the first truly global conflict, with fighting in Europe, North America, India, and at sea.

sources.name: Frederick II, Memoirs of the House of Brandenburg
Christopher Duffy, Frederick the Great: A Military Life

events.certainty_note: Invasion date of 29 August 1756 is well-documented in contemporary military records and diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Battle of Minorca and British naval defeat
events.description: On 20 May 1756, a French fleet under the Comte de la Galissonnière defeats a British squadron commanded by Admiral John Byng off Minorca. The British lose control of the island, a strategic Mediterranean possession. Byng is court-martialed and executed in 1757, becoming a scapegoat for British naval unpreparedness. The loss shocks the British public and leads to naval reforms and increased investment in fleet strength.
key_figures: Duke of Newcastle (British First Lord of the Admiralty)

sources.name: British Admiralty records and court-martial proceedings
Voltaire, Candide (satirical reference to Byng's execution)

events.certainty_note: Battle date and outcome are well-documented in naval records. Byng's execution in 1757 is historically certain.

events.title: Fort Oswego captured by French forces
events.description: In August 1756, French forces under the Marquis de Montcalm capture Fort Oswego on Lake Ontario from the British. This victory gives France control of the Great Lakes and disrupts British supply lines to the interior. The capture is part of the broader French strategy to contain British expansion in North America and secure the fur trade. The loss is a significant setback for British colonial ambitions.
key_figures: Colonel James Mercer (British commander, killed in action)
events.certainty_note: Capture date and circumstances are documented in French and British military records.

events.title: Lisbon earthquake and tsunami
events.description: On 1 November 1756 (All Saints' Day), a massive earthquake strikes Lisbon, Portugal, followed by a tsunami and fires that destroy much of the city. Estimates suggest 10,000–100,000 deaths (modern estimates tend toward the lower end). The disaster kills approximately 15% of Lisbon's population and destroys irreplaceable cultural and religious monuments. The earthquake has profound philosophical consequences, challenging Enlightenment optimism and prompting theological debate about divine providence. Reconstruction under the Marquês de Pombal becomes a model of rational urban planning.
key_figures: Marquês de Pombal (Portuguese Prime Minister, oversees reconstruction)

sources.name: Voltaire, Poem on the Lisbon Disaster
Geological and seismic analysis of the 1755 event

events.certainty_note: The earthquake occurred on 1 November 1755 (not 1756 as sometimes misreported). The date is certain from contemporary sources. Note: This event actually occurred in 1755, not 1756. Included here as a correction to common misdating.

events.title: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart born in Salzburg
events.description: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart is born on 27 January 1756 in Salzburg, Austria, to Leopold Mozart, a respected court musician and composer. Mozart's birth marks the beginning of one of history's most prolific and influential musical careers. By age five, he is performing for European courts; by his teens, he has composed symphonies, concertos, and operas. His work bridges the Classical and early Romantic periods and fundamentally shapes Western music.
key_figures: Maria Anna 'Nannerl' Mozart (sister, also a prodigy)

sources.name: Leopold Mozart's letters and family documents
Otto Erich Deutsch, Mozart: A Documentary Biography

events.certainty_note: Birth date of 27 January 1756 is documented in parish records and confirmed by Leopold Mozart's correspondence.

events.title: British capture of Calcutta from French
events.description: In 1756, British forces under Robert Clive recapture Calcutta from French control, reasserting British dominance in Bengal. This victory is part of the broader struggle for control of India during the Seven Years' War. The British East India Company's military success in India contrasts with initial setbacks in North America and Europe, establishing the foundation for British imperial expansion in South Asia.

sources.name: William Dalrymple, The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company

events.certainty_note: The recapture of Calcutta is documented, though the exact chronology of events in 1756 is complex. Clive's major victory at Plassey occurs in 1757.

disconfirming_evidence: The Lisbon earthquake is commonly dated to 1755, not 1756, though some sources misdated it to 1756 in earlier historiography. Modern seismic analysis confirms the 1 November 1755 date. The significance of the 1756 events in India is sometimes overstated; the decisive British victory came at Plassey in 1757, not in 1756. Frederick II's invasion of Saxony is sometimes portrayed as a purely aggressive act, but Prussian historians argue it was a preemptive strike against an imminent coalition attack—a claim disputed by Austrian and Russian sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1756 is exceptionally rich, dominated by European and colonial sources. Military records, diplomatic correspondence, and court documents are abundant for European and British-Indian events. However, the perspective is heavily weighted toward European powers and their colonial enterprises. Chinese, Ottoman, and indigenous American sources for this year are underrepresented in Western historiography. The narrative of the Seven Years' War is often told as a European conflict with colonial sideshows, when in fact the global dimensions were integral to the war's outcome. Enlightenment-era sources also tend to reflect elite perspectives; popular experiences of war and displacement are less well documented.

graph_edges.note: Prussian invasion initiates the Seven Years' War, which concludes with the Treaty of Hubertusburg in 1763
British naval defeat at Minorca leads to Admiral Byng's court-martial and execution in 1757
French victories in North America, including Fort Oswego, are part of the broader North American theatre that culminates at the Plains of Abraham in 1759
Mozart's birth in 1756 begins a career that transforms Western music until his death in 1791
British recapture of Calcutta in 1756 sets the stage for the decisive Battle of Plassey in 1757
